content:
6 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

I opened a repo a while back that wasn’t mine ( was stalking just for fun ), maybe fifteen or twenty files deep into folders with names that meant nothing to me, and I remember just sitting there scrolling without actually reading anything. Not because the code was hard. I could read every individual line fine.
It was that none of it connected to anything. I didn’t know why this file called that one, why there were two files doing almost the same thing, why a function had a comment that just said “fix later” with no date, no name, nothing attached to it. I closed my laptop for a bit and started listening to music, which is usually what I do when I don’t want to admit I’m stuck, and all because music is peace :)
What’s funny is I hadn’t even gotten to the actual understanding-the-code part yet. Before that there’s a whole separate layer of pain nobody warns you about. Getting the thing to even run locally. The README says one thing, the actual setup needs three env variables nobody mentioned, a specific node version you don’t have, and some service running in the background that the original developer clearly assumed everyone would just know about.
I’ve lost entire evenings to this before writing a single useful line, and there’s a specific kind of frustration in that, because it doesn’t feel like real work. You can’t show anyone what you did that day. You just fought your own machine for four hours so you could get to the starting line everyone else seems to have already crossed.
That feeling is weirdly hard to explain to someone who hasn’t had it, and even harder to explain to yourself the first time, because your instinct is to think something is wrong with you. You’ve built projects before. You know the syntax, you know the patterns, you’ve literally done everything a developer would do in their starting phase. So why does opening someone else’s code feel like you’re back in first year, staring at a problem statement you don’t fully understand ?
It’s real fun when i get to work on my personal projects. but the confidence I have when I’m writing my own code just doesn’t show up in the same way when I’m reading someone else’s. It took me way longer than it should have to even notice that asymmetry existed, because for the longest time I just assumed I was bad at “real” engineering, or whatever that meant.
When you build your own project, you hold the entire mental model in your head because you built it piece by piece. Every file makes sense because you’re the one who decided where it goes. Every function has context because you remember why you wrote it that way. There’s no gap between the code and your understanding of the code, because they grew together.
A real codebase doesn’t give you that. You’re dropped into the middle of someone else’s mental model, except that model was never written down anywhere, it just lives in the heads of whoever built the thing, half of whom have probably already left the company.
You have to reverse engineer intent from implementation. You have to guess why a certain function is doing something weird, whether it’s a bug, a workaround for some edge case nobody documented, or just bad code that never got cleaned up. This is a completely different skill from writing code from scratch, and it’s one that almost nobody explicitly teaches you.
I remember the first time I had to actually trace a bug through a codebase I didn’t write. It wasn’t dramatic, there was no eureka moment like we do in personal project like — “yayyyyy”. It was just slow, uncomfortable, and honestly kind of humbling. I kept opening files that seemed relevant and closing them because they weren’t. I kept building little theories about what was wrong and watching them fall apart.
What struck me afterward wasn’t the bug itself, but how little my prior experience had prepared me for that specific kind of discomfort. Years of building projects, and this still felt like starting over.
I don’t think this is a sign that self-taught developers or CS students are somehow deficient. I actually think it says more about how we learn than about the people learning. Courses and tutorials are built around a very specific shape of problem: here’s a clean starting point, here’s the goal, follow these steps and you’ll get there. Even personal projects, as valuable as they are, follow this same shape because you’re the one defining the starting point and the goal.
Real engineering work almost never looks like this. You inherit half finished systems. You inherit decisions someone else made for reasons that may no longer be valid. The problem you’re solving is rarely stated clearly, you have to figure out what the actual problem even is before you can start solving it.
So the knowledge you built through tutorials and courses isn’t wrong, it’s just incomplete in a specific direction. It teaches you how to create. It doesn’t teach you how to navigate. And navigation, weirdly, is most of what the job actually is once you’re past the first few weeks of any real engineering role.
There’s also a quieter part of this that I don’t see people talk about much, which is how isolating it feels in the moment. You’re in a call or a thread with people who seem to move through the same codebase without any of this friction, asking sharp questions, dropping context you don’t have, and you start assuming everyone else just gets it faster than you do.
So you don’t ask the obvious question. You go quiet and try to figure it out alone, partly out of ego and partly because you don’t want the one question that reveals how lost you actually are. I’ve done this more times than I’d like to admit, spent hours stuck on something a thirty second question would have solved, because asking felt like admitting I didn’t belong there yet.
What I keep coming back to is that this is less about skill and more about exposure. Nobody sits you down and makes you read fifty thousand lines of unfamiliar code before your first job. Nobody hands you a codebase with a subtle bug buried three layers deep and says, find this, and by the way, nobody’s going to tell you exactly where to look.
That kind of exposure is usually something you only get once you’re already inside a company, learning it on the job, often while being quietly judged for how long it takes you.
I don’t think the problem is that we don’t know how to code.
If anything, most of us spend years trying to get better at it. We build projects, complete courses, explore new technologies, and slowly become comfortable turning ideas into working software. None of that effort is wasted.
The gap appears later, much later… either in the first week of job, first month of open source or just casually stalking someone’s else code.
It also appears when you’re asked to work inside something you didn’t create. When the challenge isn’t writing a feature from scratch, but understanding why an existing one behaves the way it does. When you’re expected to make a change without breaking three other things you didn’t even know were connected.
That’s a different muscle and for all the obvious reasons ppl find it quite hard… ( vibe coding )
The strange part is that we’re expected to develop it only after we join a team, often while someone is waiting for us to become productive and maybe that’s why so many developers walk into their first engineering role feeling like they suddenly became worse at programming.
They did not obviously. They’re just encountering a part of software engineering they were never really given the chance to practice.
